title: You Just Had to Make Something: An Interview with Harry Dodge & Silas Howard
description: "Because success was nowhere in sight, we kind of couldn't fail. There were no expectations; nobody was going to do anything for us. We could complain, but who would listen? You just had to make something. So we did! We made our own cafés, our own bands, our own record labels, our own movies, and it was great."
hook: Two queer artists built cafés, bands, and film festivals from nothing because failure wasn't an option—a manifesto disguised as an interview.

title: What Is Poetry? Chelsey Minnis’s Frying Pan Full of Diamonds
description: “For the past twenty-five years, Chelsey Minnis has waged a sustained assault on the ideology of poetic sincerity.”
hook: Chelsey Minnis spent 25 years demolishing poetic sincerity, one deliberately awkward line at a time. Why that matters now.

title: I Glimpsed My Future at a High School Speech Tournament
description:
Original Oratory by James Davis In every speech kid, I see a version of myself that I love with frightening intensity: 14 years old, a closeted freshman at Liberty High School with long curly Weird Al Yankovic hair, thick glasses, blisteringly cheap dress shoes, and something to say.  It was the second week of the […]

The post I Glimpsed My Future at a High School Speech Tournam
hook: A closeted speech kid sees his 14-year-old self in every competitor—and can't stop loving him with terrifying intensity.

title: Who Owns Argentine Football?
description:
In early 20th century Buenos Aires, football clubs emerged as institutions of sociability and mutual aid, but also of anarchist resistance, immigrant solidarity, and antifascist organizing.

The post Who Owns Argentine Football? appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Buenos Aires' football clubs were never just about the game—they were anarchist resistance cells, immigrant sanctuaries, and antifascist organizing hubs.

title: Sarah Schulman’s Four Decades of Lesbian Fiction
description: “Nothing stops me except the publishing industry,” quipped the novelist and AIDS historian, who cut her teeth as an East Village journalist writing for queer and feminist papers.
hook: Sarah Schulman spent four decades writing lesbian fiction the publishing industry never wanted—until it suddenly did.
